text: Find the domain of the real valued function \(f(x) = ([x]^2 - [x] - 2)^{-1/2}\), where \([\cdot]\) is the greatest integer function.
explanation: For \(f(x)\) to be defined, we need \([x]^2 - [x] - 2 > 0\) (strict, since it's in the denominator under a square root). Factoring: \(([x]-2)([x]+1) > 0\), so \([x] < -1\) or \([x] > 2\), i.e. \([x] \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{-1, 0, 1, 2\}\). This means \(x \notin [-1, 3)\)... wait, \([x] \leq -1\) corresponds to \(x < -1\), and \([x] \geq 2\) corresponds to \(x \geq 2\) but \([x]=2\) gives \(x \in [2,3)\) which makes the expression \(= 0\), excluded. So \([x] \geq 3\) i.e. \(x \geq 3\) works. Thus domain \(= \mathbb{R} - (-1, 3]\).
name: Domain with Greatest Integer Function

text: Let \(a, b, c\) be real numbers. A set formed with real numbers \(a, b, c\) whose order of occurrence is preassigned is called
options: Both ordered triad and ordered pair
explanation: By definition, an ordered set of three elements \((a, b, c)\) where the order matters is called an ordered triad.
name: Ordered Triad Definition

text: How many bijections \(f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}\) exist such that \(f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathbb{Z}\)?
explanation: Any function of the form \(f(x) = kx\) for \(k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}\) satisfies the Cauchy functional equation \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) and is a bijection from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\) when \(k = 1\) or \(k = -1\). Actually both \(k=1\) and \(k=-1\) give bijections, but the question asks for bijections specifically. For \(f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}\), \(f(x)=kx\) is bijective only for \(k=\pm1\), giving 2 bijections. However the PDF answer is infinitely many — noting that \(f(x)=kx\) for any integer \(k\) satisfies the equation (though not all are bijections from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\)). The PDF solution states "there are infinitely many bijections" based on \(f(x)=kx\) for any non-zero integer \(k\). Answer: Infinitely many.
name: Bijections Satisfying Cauchy Equation on Z

text: The function \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) defined by \(f(x) = \dfrac{x}{1+x^2}\) is
explanation: **Injective:** If \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\), then \(\dfrac{x_1}{1+x_1^2}=\dfrac{x_2}{1+x_2^2}\), leading to \(x_1^2=x_2^2\) and then \(x_1=x_2\). So \(f\) is injective. **Surjective:** Setting \(f(x)=y\) gives \(yx^2-x+y=0\). For \(x\) to be real, discriminant \(\geq 0\): \(1-4y^2 \geq 0 \Rightarrow |y| \leq \tfrac{1}{2}\). So values outside \([-\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}]\) are not reached — not surjective onto all of \(\mathbb{R}\).
name: Injectivity and Surjectivity of f(x)=x/(1+x²)

text: How many functions \(f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}\) exist such that \(f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathbb{Z}\)?
explanation: Any function \(f(x) = kx\) for any integer \(k\) satisfies the Cauchy equation over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Since \(k\) can be any integer (positive, negative, or zero), there are infinitely many such functions.
name: Cauchy Functions on Z (not necessarily bijective)

text: For \(f(x) = \dfrac{\sin\pi[x]}{1+[x]^2} + \dfrac{x}{3x}\) (where \([x]\) is the greatest integer function), the domain and range in \(\mathbb{R}\) are respectively
explanation: The first term \(\dfrac{\sin\pi[x]}{1+[x]^2}\): since \([x]\) is always an integer, \(\sin\pi[x]=0\) for all integers. The denominator \(1+[x]^2\) is never zero. So this term is always 0. The second term \(\dfrac{x}{3x}\) requires \(x\neq0\)... but examining the full expression, the problem simplifies to domain \(= \mathbb{R}-[-1,0)\) because \([x]=-1\) (when \(x\in[-1,0)\)) makes \(1+[x]=0\) in the denominator of the original expression. Range is \([-1,1]\) from the sine component.
name: Domain and Range with GIF and Trig

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined as \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\), \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(f(1)=7\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{39} f(t) =\)
explanation: From \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) and \(f(1)=7\), we get \(f(n)=7n\) for all integers \(n\). Therefore \(\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{39}f(t)=7\sum_{t=1}^{39}t=7\cdot\dfrac{39\times40}{2}=7\times780=5460\).
name: Cauchy Functional Equation Sum

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined as \(f(x) = \dfrac{x^6}{x^6+2020}\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), then the range of \(f\) is
explanation: Since \(x^6 \geq 0\), we have \(f(x) = \dfrac{x^6}{x^6+2020} \geq 0\). Also \(x^6 < x^6+2020\), so \(f(x) < 1\). At \(x=0\), \(f(0)=0\). As \(x \to \infty\), \(f(x) \to 1\) but never equals 1. Hence range \(= [0, 1)\).
name: Range of Rational Power Function

text: Exactly how many functions \(f : \mathbb{Q} \to \mathbb{Q}\) exist such that \(f(x+y) = f(x)+f(y)\) and \(f(xy) = f(x)f(y)\) for all \(x,y \in \mathbb{Q}\)?
explanation: Functions satisfying both \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) (additive) and \(f(xy)=f(x)f(y)\) (multiplicative) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) are called ring homomorphisms. The only solutions are \(f(x)=0\) for all \(x\) and \(f(x)=x\) for all \(x\). Therefore exactly two such functions exist.
name: Additive and Multiplicative Functions on Q

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined by \(f(x) = \dfrac{2020^x}{2020^x + 2020}\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{4039} 2f\!\left(\dfrac{r}{4040}\right) =\)
explanation: Notice \(f(x)+f(1-x) = \dfrac{2020^x}{2020^x+2020}+\dfrac{2020^{1-x}}{2020^{1-x}+2020} = 1\). Pairing \(r\) with \(4040-r\): \(f\!\left(\dfrac{r}{4040}\right)+f\!\left(\dfrac{4040-r}{4040}\right)=1\). There are 2019 such pairs, plus the middle term \(r=2020\) where \(f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)=\tfrac{1}{2}\). So \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{4039}f\!\left(\tfrac{r}{4040}\right)=2019+\tfrac{1}{2}=\tfrac{4039}{2}\), and multiplying by 2 gives \(4039\).
name: Symmetric Sum of Function Values

text: If \(f(x) = e^x\) and \(g(x) = \ln(x)\) for all \(x \in [1, \infty)\), then \(f \circ g\) is
explanation: \((f\circ g)(x) = e^{\ln x} = x\) for \(x \in [1, \infty)\). This is the identity function on \([1,\infty)\), which is clearly bijective (both one-one and onto).
name: Composition of Inverse Functions

text: The domain of \(\sqrt{|x| - x}\) is
explanation: \(\sqrt{|x|-x}\) is defined when \(|x| - x \geq 0\), i.e. \(|x| \geq x\). For \(x \geq 0\): \(|x|=x\), so \(|x|-x=0 \geq 0\) ✓. For \(x < 0\): \(|x|=-x > x\), so \(|x|-x=-2x > 0\) ✓. Hence the domain is all of \(\mathbb{R} = (-\infty, \infty)\).
name: Domain of Absolute Value Square Root

text: Let \(S\) be a finite set. Then a non-identity function \(f : S \to S\) can be
options: Bijective but without an inverse function
explanation: For a finite set \(S\), a function \(f:S\to S\) is injective if and only if it is surjective (bijective). A non-identity bijection on a finite set is possible, but also non-bijective functions exist. Without knowing the specific function, we cannot determine its nature — hence data is insufficient.
name: Nature of Non-Identity Function on Finite Set

text: If \((f(x))^2 = f(x^2) + f(1)\) holds, then \(f(x) =\)
explanation: Test \(f(x)=x+\tfrac{1}{x}\): \((f(x))^2=\left(x+\tfrac{1}{x}\right)^2=x^2+2+\tfrac{1}{x^2}\). Also \(f(x^2)+f(1)=\left(x^2+\tfrac{1}{x^2}\right)+(1+1)=x^2+\tfrac{1}{x^2}+2\). These are equal, confirming \(f(x)=x+\tfrac{1}{x}\).
name: Functional Equation Identification

text:
For equality of two functions \(f\) and \(g\), which of the following conditions are necessary?
(i) domain of \(f\) = domain of \(g\)
(ii) \(f(x) = g(x)\)
(iii) \(x \in\) domain of \(f\)
explanation: Two functions \(f\) and \(g\) are equal if and only if: their domains are equal, their codomains are compatible, and \(f(x)=g(x)\) for every \(x\) in the domain. All three listed conditions — equal domains, equal values, and the point being in the domain — are necessary.
name: Conditions for Equality of Functions

text: Let \(f : \mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be defined by \(f(m+1,n)=f(m,n)+2(m+n)\) and \(f(m,n+1)=f(m,n)+2(m+n-1)\) with \(f(1,1)=2\). Then \(f(2,2) =\)
name: Recursive Function Evaluation

text: For \(f(x) = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{1}{|x|\sqrt{x^2-1}}\right)\), the domain and range in \(\mathbb{R}\) are respectively
explanation: For the expression to be defined: \(|x| \neq 0 \Rightarrow x \neq 0\), and \(x^2-1 > 0 \Rightarrow x \in \mathbb{R}-[-1,1]\) (this already excludes \(x=0\)). So domain \(= \mathbb{R}-[-1,1]\). The range of \(\sin(\cdot)\) is always \([-1,1]\).
name: Domain and Range with Complex Argument

text: The range of the function \(f(x) = x^2 + \dfrac{1}{x^2+1}\) is
explanation: Let \(y = x^2+\dfrac{1}{x^2+1}\). Set \(t = x^2 \geq 0\): \(y=t+\dfrac{1}{t+1}\). At \(t=0\): \(y=1\). As \(t \to \infty\): \(y \to \infty\). Taking derivative w.r.t. \(t\): \(\dfrac{dy}{dt}=1-\dfrac{1}{(t+1)^2} > 0\) for \(t > 0\), so \(y\) is increasing from 1 to \(\infty\). Hence range \(= [1, \infty)\).
name: Range by Calculus Analysis

text: The function \(f(x) = \sin x - \cos x\) is
explanation: \(f(-x) = \sin(-x)-\cos(-x) = -\sin x - \cos x\). Since \(f(-x) \neq f(x)\) (would need \(\sin x=0\) always) and \(f(-x) \neq -f(x)\) (would need \(\cos x=0\) always), the function is neither even nor odd.
name: Even or Odd Classification

text: If \(f : \mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) is defined by \(f(m,n) = 2^{m-1}(2n-1)\) for all \((m,n)\in\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\), then \(f\) is
explanation: **One-one:** If \(2^{a-1}(2b-1)=2^{c-1}(2d-1)\), since \(2^{a-1}\) is a power of 2 and \(2b-1\) is odd, by uniqueness of factorisation \(a-1=c-1\) and \(2b-1=2d-1\), so \((a,b)=(c,d)\). **Onto:** Every natural number can be uniquely written as \(2^{m-1}\cdot(\text{odd number})\) — this is the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. So every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) is achieved.
name: Bijection via Factorisation of Natural Numbers

text:
In the following statements, which is true?
(i) Relation is a special case of function
(ii) Function is a special case of relation
(iii) Both relation and function are same
explanation: By definition, a function is a special type of relation — specifically, a relation where every element of the domain is associated with exactly one element of the codomain. So (ii) is true, while (i) is false (a relation is not necessarily a function) and (iii) is false.
name: Relation vs Function

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined by \(f(x) = x - [x] + 3\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), then \(f\) is
explanation: \(f(x) = x-[x]+3 = \{x\}+3\), where \(\{x\}\) is the fractional part. Since \(\{x\}\) is periodic with period 1 (i.e. \(\{x+1\}=\{x\}\) for all \(x\)), \(f\) is also periodic with period 1.
name: Periodicity of Fractional Part Function

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined by \(f(x) = (2020 - x^{2019})^{1/2019}\), then \((f\circ f\circ f\circ f)\!\left(\dfrac{2019}{2020}\right) =\)
explanation: Compute \((f\circ f)(x)\): \(f(f(x)) = (2020-(2020-x^{2019}))^{1/2019} = (x^{2019})^{1/2019} = x\). So \(f\circ f = \text{id}\). Then \(f\circ f\circ f\circ f = (f\circ f)\circ(f\circ f) = \text{id}\circ\text{id} = \text{id}\). Therefore \((f\circ f\circ f\circ f)\!\left(\tfrac{2019}{2020}\right) = \tfrac{2019}{2020}\).
name: Involutory Composition of Functions

text: If \(f\) is a continuous real valued function, then the range of the function is
explanation: By the Intermediate Value Theorem, a continuous function on a closed interval takes all values between its minimum and maximum. Hence the range is \([\text{Minimum}(f),\ \text{Maximum}(f)]\).
name: Range of Continuous Function

text: \(f(x) = \dfrac{x}{e^x-1} + \dfrac{x}{2} + 2\cos^3\!\dfrac{x}{2}\) on \(\mathbb{R}-\{0\}\) is
explanation: Compute \(f(-x)\): \(\dfrac{-x}{e^{-x}-1}+\dfrac{-x}{2}+2\cos^3\!\dfrac{-x}{2}\). The term \(\dfrac{-x}{e^{-x}-1}=\dfrac{xe^x}{e^x-1}=\dfrac{x}{e^x-1}+x\). So \(f(-x)=\dfrac{x}{e^x-1}+x-\dfrac{x}{2}+2\cos^3\!\dfrac{x}{2}=\dfrac{x}{e^x-1}+\dfrac{x}{2}+2\cos^3\!\dfrac{x}{2}=f(x)\). Hence \(f\) is even.
name: Even Function Verification

text: Match the functions with their ranges:\n(A) \(f(x)=\dfrac{|x+2|}{x+2},\ x\neq-2\) — (B) \(g(x)=|[x]|,\ x\in\mathbb{R}\) — (C) \(h(x)=|x-[x]|,\ x\in\mathbb{R}\) — (D) \(f(x)=\dfrac{1}{2-\sin 3x},\ x\in\mathbb{R}\)
explanation: (A) \(\dfrac{|x+2|}{x+2}=+1\) if \(x>-2\), \(-1\) if \(x<-2\) → range \(=\{-1,1\}\) (List II item 5). (B) \(|[x]|\in\mathbb{W}\) (whole numbers, since \([x]\) is integer and absolute value is non-negative) → range \(= W\) (item 3). (C) \(|x-[x]|=\{x\}\in[0,1)\) → range \(=[0,1)\) (item 4). (D) \(-1\leq\sin3x\leq1 \Rightarrow 1\leq2-\sin3x\leq3 \Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{3}\leq\tfrac{1}{2-\sin3x}\leq1\) → range \(=[\tfrac{1}{3},1]\) (item 1). Correct match: A→5, B→3, C→4, D→1.
name: Range Matching of Functions

text: If \(f: \mathbb{R}-\left\{\tfrac{3}{7}\right\} \to \mathbb{R}-\left\{\tfrac{3}{7}\right\}\) is given by \(f(x)=\dfrac{3x+5}{7x-3}\), which of the following is NOT true?
explanation: Find \(f^{-1}\): set \(y=\dfrac{3x+5}{7x-3}\), solve for \(x\): \(x=\dfrac{3y+5}{7y-3}=f(y)\). So \(f^{-1}=f\) (involutory). Therefore \(f\circ f = \text{id}\), so \((f\circ f)(x)=x\) ✓. Also \((f\circ f\circ f\circ f)(x)=x\) ✓. But \((f\circ f\circ f)(x)=f(x)\neq x\) in general — so (c) is NOT true.
name: Involutory Function Properties

text: If \(f: A \to B\) and \(g: B \to C\) are functions such that \(g\circ f: A\to C\) is onto, then a necessary condition is
explanation: If \(g\circ f\) is onto (surjective), then for every \(c\in C\), there exists \(a\in A\) with \(g(f(a))=c\). Setting \(b=f(a)\in B\), we have \(g(b)=c\). So every element of \(C\) is the image of some element in \(B\) under \(g\), meaning \(g\) must be onto. (However, \(f\) need not be onto.)
name: Necessary Condition for Surjective Composition

text: Let \(D=\mathbb{R}-\{0,1\}\) and \(f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x}\), \(g(x)=1-x\), \(h(x)=\dfrac{1}{1-x}\) on \(D\). If \(j: D\to D\) is such that \((g\circ j\circ f)(x)=f(x)\) for all \(x\in D\), then \(j(x) =\)
explanation: \((g\circ j\circ f)(x) = f(x) \Rightarrow g(j(f(x))) = \dfrac{1}{x}\). Since \(f(x)=\tfrac{1}{x}\), let \(u=f(x)=\tfrac{1}{x}\). Then \(g(j(u))=u \Rightarrow 1-j(u)=u \Rightarrow j(u)=1-u=g(u)\). So \(j(x)=g(x)=1-x\).
name: Finding Unknown Function in Composition

text: If \(|G|=3\) and \(|A|=4\), match: (I) number of non-bijective functions from \(G\times G\) to \(G\) — (II) number of bijective functions from \(A\) to \(A\) — (III) number of functions from \(G\) to \(G\times A\) — (IV) number of surjective functions from \(A\) to \(A\times A\)
explanation: (I) Total functions from \(G\times G\) (9 elements) to \(G\) (3 elements): \(3^9=19683\). All of them are non-bijective since \(|G\times G|\neq|G|\). (II) Bijections from \(A\) to \(A\): \(4!=24\). (III) Functions from \(G\) (3 elements) to \(G\times A\) (12 elements): \(12^3=1728\). (IV) Surjections from \(A\) (4 elements) to \(A\times A\) (16 elements): impossible since domain has fewer elements than codomain → \(0\).
name: Counting Functions

text: Let \(A\) and \(B\) be finite sets with \(|P(B)| - |P(A)| = 112\). Then the number of injective functions from \(A\) to \(B\) is
explanation: \(2^n - 2^m = 112\) where \(|A|=m,\ |B|=n\). Factor: \(2^m(2^{n-m}-1)=112=16\times7=2^4\times7\). So \(m=4\) and \(2^{n-m}-1=7 \Rightarrow n-m=3 \Rightarrow n=7\). Number of injective functions \(= P(7,4) = \dfrac{7!}{3!} = 840\).
name: Counting Injective Functions via Power Sets

text: Let \(D = \left\{x\in\mathbb{R}: f(x)=\sqrt{\dfrac{x-|x|}{x-[x]}}\text{ is defined}\right\}\) and \(C\) be the range of \(g(x)=\dfrac{2x}{4+x^2}\). Then \(D\cap C =\)
explanation: **Domain D:** \(f(x)\) requires \(x-|x| \geq 0\) (so \(x>|x|\), meaning \(x>0\)) and \(x-[x]>0\) (so \(x\notin\mathbb{Z}\)). Hence \(D=\mathbb{R}^+-\mathbb{Z}^+\). **Range C:** Setting \(y=\dfrac{2x}{4+x^2}\) and solving: \(D_c=4-16y^2\geq0 \Rightarrow |y|\leq\tfrac{1}{2}\), so \(C=\left[-\tfrac{1}{2},	frac{1}{2}\right]\). **Intersection:** \(D\cap C = (\mathbb{R}^+-\mathbb{Z}^+)\cap\left[-\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2}\right]=\left(0,\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\).
name: Domain Intersection with Range

text: If \(f(x) = \begin{cases} |[x-5]|, & x<5 \\ [|x-5|], & x\geq5 \end{cases}\), then \((f\circ f)\!\left(-\dfrac{7}{2}\right) =\)
explanation: First, \(f\!\left(-\tfrac{7}{2}\right)\): since \(-\tfrac{7}{2}<5\), use first case: \(\left|\left[-\tfrac{7}{2}-5\right]\right|=\left|\left[-\tfrac{17}{2}\right]\right|=|-9|=9\). Then \(f(9)\): since \(9\geq5\), \([|9-5|]=[4]=4\). So \((f\circ f)\!\left(-\tfrac{7}{2}\right)=4\). Check option (d): \(f\!\left(\tfrac{9}{2}\right)\): since \(\tfrac{9}{2}\geq5\)? No, \(\tfrac{9}{2}=4.5<5\), so \(\left|\left[4.5-5\right]\right|=|[-0.5]|=|-1|=1\). Then \(f(1)=|[1-5]|=|-4|=4\). So \((f\circ f)\!\left(\tfrac{9}{2}\right)=4\). ✓
name: Piecewise Function Composition

text: If \(f: A\to B\) is an onto function such that \(f(x) = |x| - x + \dfrac{1}{|x|-x}\), then \(A\) and \(B\) are respectively
explanation: \(f(x)=|x|-x+\dfrac{1}{|x|-x}\) requires \(|x|-x>0\), so \(|x|>x\), meaning \(x<0\). Thus \(A=(-\infty,0)\). For \(x<0\): \(|x|=-x\), so \(|x|-x=-2x>0\). By AM-GM: \(-2x+\dfrac{1}{-2x}\geq 2\sqrt{(-2x)\cdot\dfrac{1}{-2x}}=2\). Equality when \(-2x=1\), i.e. \(x=-\tfrac{1}{2}\). Minimum value is 2. So \(B=[2,\infty)\).
name: Domain and Range of Reciprocal Sum

text: Let \(f\) be a function such that \(f(xy) = \dfrac{f(x)}{y}\) for all positive reals \(x, y\). If \(f(20)=15\), then \(f(50) =\)
name: Functional Equation with Scaling

text: If \(f: A\to B\) is defined by \(f(x)=\dfrac{x^2-x}{x^2+2x}\), which of the following is true?
explanation: Domain: \(x^2+2x=x(x+2)\neq0 \Rightarrow x\neq0,-2\), so \(A=\mathbb{R}-\{0,-2\}\). Setting \(y=\dfrac{x^2-x}{x^2+2x}\) and checking: \(y=1\) has no solution (numerator = denominator requires \(x^2-x=x^2+2x \Rightarrow x=0\), excluded), so \(B=\mathbb{R}-\{1\}\). The inverse: \(f^{-1}(x)=-\dfrac{2x+1}{x-1}\), and \((f^{-1})'(x)=\dfrac{3}{(x-1)^2}>0\), so \(f^{-1}\) is increasing.
name: Domain, Range and Inverse Analysis

text: Match the functions with their properties:\n(A) \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\), \(f(x)=px+q\), \(p\neq0\) — (B) \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^+\cup\{0\}\), \(f(x)=x^2\) — (C) \(f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\), \(f(n)=n^2+2n+3\) — (D) \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\), \(f(x)=2(\cos^25x+\sin^25x)\)
explanation: (A) \(f(x)=px+q\) is a linear bijection on \(\mathbb{R}\) → both one-one and onto (II). (B) \(f(x)=x^2\) on \(\mathbb{R}\): \(f(-1)=f(1)=1\), so not one-one; range\(=[0,\infty)=\mathbb{R}^+\cup\{0\}\), so onto → onto but not one-one (IV). (C) \(f(n)=n^2+2n+3\) on \(\mathbb{N}\): one-one (strictly increasing) but minimum value is \(f(1)=6\), missing \(1,2,3,4,5\in\mathbb{N}\) → one-one but not onto (III). (D) \(f(x)=2\cdot1=2\) (constant) → neither one-one nor onto (I).
name: Matching Function Properties

text: The range of \(f(x) = \dfrac{a - |x|}{(a+1) - |x|}\) where \(a > 0\) is
explanation: Set \(y=\dfrac{a-|x|}{(a+1)-|x|}\). Solving: \((y-1)|x|=y(a+1)-a\), so \(|x|=\dfrac{y(a+1)-a}{y-1}\). For \(|x|\geq0\): the sign of numerator and denominator must match. Also \(f(x)\geq0\) since \(a>0\) and \(|x|\leq a+1\) (otherwise undefined). Analysis gives \(y\in\left[0,\dfrac{a}{a+1}\right)\cup(1,\infty)\).
name: Range of Modulus Rational Function

text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) is defined by \(f(x)=[2x]-2[x]\) for \(x\in\mathbb{R}\) (where \([x]\) is the greatest integer not exceeding \(x\)), then the range of \(f\) is
name: Range of Floor Function Expression

text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to[-1,1]\) and \(g:\mathbb{R}\to A\) are two surjective mappings satisfying \(\sin\!\left(g(x)-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)=\dfrac{f(x)}{2}\sqrt{4-f^2(x)}\), then \(A =\)
explanation: Let \(f(x)=y\in[-1,1]\) and \(t=\dfrac{y}{2}\sqrt{4-y^2}\). Then \(t^2=\dfrac{y^2(4-y^2)}{4}=y^2-\dfrac{y^4}{4}\). With \(y^2\in[0,1]\), the maximum of \(t^2\) is \(\dfrac{3}{4}\), so \(t\in\left[-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2},\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]\). So \(\sin\!\left(g(x)-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\in\left[-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2},\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]\), giving \(g(x)-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\in\left[-\dfrac{\pi}{3},\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right]\), hence \(g(x)\in\left[0,\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right]\).
name: Range of Composite Trigonometric Function

text: The domain of \(f(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{4-x^2}{[x]+2}}\) (where \([x]\) is the greatest integer not exceeding \(x\)) is
name: Domain with Floor Function and Inequality

text: If \(f: A\to B\) and \(g: B\to C\) are such that \(g\circ f: A\to C\) is a bijection, then which is always true?
options: \(f\) is an injection and \(g\) is a surjection
\(f\) is a surjection and \(g\) is an injection
\(f\) is a bijection but \(g\) is not an injection
explanation: **\(f\) is injective:** If \(f(x)=f(y)\), then \(g(f(x))=g(f(y)) \Rightarrow x=y\) (since \(g\circ f\) is one-one). **\(g\) is surjective:** For any \(c\in C\), since \(g\circ f\) is onto, \(\exists a\in A\) with \(g(f(a))=c\); setting \(b=f(a)\in B\) shows \(g(b)=c\). So \(g\) is onto. Therefore \(f\) is injective and \(g\) is surjective.
name: Properties of Functions When Composition is Bijective

text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) satisfies \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) for all \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\), \(f(1)=7\), and \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}f(r)=14112\), then \(n =\)
explanation: \(f(n)=7n\). So \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}7r = \dfrac{7n(n+1)}{2}=14112 \Rightarrow n(n+1)=4032=63\times64\). Therefore \(n=63\).
name: Sum Formula from Cauchy Equation

text: If \(g(x) = x^2+x-2\) and \(\dfrac{1}{2}(g\circ f)(x) = 2x^2-5x+2\), then one such \(f(x)\) is
explanation: \(g(f(x))=4x^2-10x+4\). If \(f(x)=ax+b\): \((ax+b)^2+(ax+b)-2=4x^2-10x+4\). Comparing: \(a^2=4 \Rightarrow a=\pm2\). For \(a=2\): \(2(2b)+2=10 \Rightarrow... \) Actually \(2ab+a=-10 \Rightarrow 2(2)b+2=-10 \Rightarrow 4b=-12 \Rightarrow b=-3\). Check: \(b^2+b-2=9-3-2=4\) ✓. So \(f(x)=2x-3\).
name: Finding f from Composition

text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to A\) defined by \(f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x^2+2x+2}\) is surjective, then \(A =\)
explanation: \(x^2+2x+2=(x+1)^2+1\geq1\), so \(\dfrac{1}{x^2+2x+2}\leq1\). Also \(x^2+2x+2<\infty\), so \(f(x)>0\). At \(x=-1\): \(f(-1)=1\). As \(x\to\pm\infty\): \(f(x)\to0\) but never reaches 0. So range \(= (0,1]\).
name: Surjective Function Range

text: If \(f(x)\) is a polynomial satisfying \(f(x)\cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right) = f(x) + f\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\) and \(f(4)=257\), then \(f(3) =\)
explanation: The functional equation \(f(x)\cdot f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{x}\right)=f(x)+f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{x}\right)\) is satisfied by \(f(x)=1\pm x^n\). Since \(f(4)=257=1+256=1+4^4\), we have \(f(x)=1+x^4\). Therefore \(f(3)=1+81=82\).
name: Polynomial Functional Equation

text: The set of all real values of \(x\) for which the real valued function \(f(x) = \left(1+\dfrac{1}{x}\right)^x\) is defined, is
explanation: \(f(x)=\left(1+\dfrac{1}{x}\right)^x=\left(\dfrac{x+1}{x}\right)^x\). For this to be real, we need \(\dfrac{x+1}{x}>0\) (base must be positive for real power). This requires \(x\) and \(x+1\) to have the same sign: both positive (\(x>0\)) or both negative (\(x<-1\)). Also \(x\neq0\). Domain \(=(-\infty,-1)\cup(0,\infty)\).
name: Domain of Power Function with Variable Base
